patterns_winning: Short, punchy titles under 10 words with a clear cause-and-effect frame ('How X is doing Y to you')
Hooks use second-person 'you' to make the threat feel immediate and personal
Winning hooks contrast a casual action with a catastrophic hidden consequence ('YOU WORE IT ONCE' vs planetary destruction)
Topics target everyday behaviors the viewer already does — wearing clothes, using a phone — making the revelation unavoidable
Language stays visceral and active: 'poisoning,' 'rewiring' — present tense damage happening right now
patterns_losing: Overly long, clinical titles lose algorithmic punch and feel like essays, not revelations
Hooks that state conclusions outright ('YOUR GUT IS ALREADY FAILING') remove suspense and kill click motivation
Topics about systemic issues (housing market, gut health) feel distant or already-known, reducing perceived novelty
High engagement but low views suggests niche resonance without broad discovery — topic lacked mass-appeal entry point
Zero-view content likely buried by algorithm due to low watch-time signals, suggesting hook failed to retain past the first second
topics: How your drinking water is quietly loading your blood with forever chemicals right now.
How your mattress is poisoning eight hours of your life every single night.
How streaming algorithms are systematically eroding your ability to make decisions.
How the air inside your home is statistically more toxic than the air outside.
How your credit score was designed to keep you financially trapped forever.
